drial biogenetical activities This article explains a fast and simple method for the puriﬁcation of mam-malian mitochondria from different tissues and cultured cells, that is suitable for the analysis of many
aspects of the organelle’s biogenesis The mitochondria isolated following the protocol described here,
are highly active and capable of DNA, RNA and protein synthesis Mitochondrial tRNA aminoacylation,mtDNA–protein interactions and speciﬁc import of added proteins into the organelles, can also be studiedusing this kind of preparations /C2112009 Elsevier B.V and Mitochondria Research Society All rights reserved 1 Introduction
Mitochondria are key organelles as their main function is to
synthesize most of the cell’s ATP via the oxidative phosphorylation
(OXPHOS) system In addition, they are involved in many other
metabolic pathways including nucleotide precursor biosynthesis,
cofactor biosynthesis, metal ion homeostasis and are also central
in the apoptotic process Animal mitochondria contain their own genome in which 13
polypeptides, all of them structural subunits of the OXPHOS Com-
plexes I, III, IV and V, are encoded The information for the rRNAs
and tRNAs necessary for the translation of these polypeptides
within the organelle is also contained in the mitochondrial DNA
(mtDNA) The rest of the complexes structural subunits and the
factors involved in mtDNA expression, as well as many other pro-
teins with diverse functions, are encoded in the nucleus and must
be imported inside mitochondria Mitochondrial dysfunction, of
mitochondrial or nuclear genetic origin, is the cause of human
disorders with a wide range of clinical presentations (Zeviani andCarelli, 2007; Zeviani and Di Donato, 2004 ) Moreover, malfunction
of mitochondria has been also associated to neurodegenerative dis-eases (Petrozzi et al., 2007 ), diabetes (Mulder and Ling, 2008 ) and
cancer (Kroemer, 2006 ) However, the mechanisms involved in all
these diseases are to a great extent still unknown The availability
of quality mitochondrial preparations is very useful in the study of
the processes underlying these pathologies as well as in the basic
knowledge of mitochondrial functionality The use of isolated mitochondria has proved to be a very pow-
erful tool in the study of the OXPHOS system biogenesis under con-
ditions resembling the in vivo enzyme/substrate proportions, ionic
composition, and integrated activity of the metabolic processes(Enriquez et al., 1999b, 1996b ) In addition, they allow a better
control and manipulability of the experimental conditions thanwhen using intact cells or tissues Isolated mitochondria preserve
the ability to synthesize DNA, RNA and proteins This capacity
has been shown to be predetermined by the metabolic state of
the cell before the mitochondria were isolated like, for example,
by the thyroid state of the animal (Enriquez et al., 1999a; Fernan-
dez-Vizarra et al., 2008 ) Many procedures for the isolation of mammalian mitochondria

2007 ) These methods are similar to the one presented herein,
which we have been using successfully for some years now The

mitochondria.protocol:
**Title: Isolation of mitochondria for biogenetical studies: An update**

**Introduction:**
- Mitochondria are key organelles involved in ATP synthesis and various metabolic pathways.
- Animal mitochondria have their own genome encoding polypeptides, rRNAs, and tRNAs.
- Other structural subunits and factors involved in mtDNA expression are encoded in the nucleus.
- Mitochondrial dysfunction is associated with human disorders and diseases.
- Isolated mitochondria are useful for studying mitochondrial functionality and pathologies.

**Steps:**
1. "This article explains a fast and simple method for the purification of mammalian mitochondria from different tissues and cultured cells, that is suitable for the analysis of many aspects of the organelle’s biogenesis."
2. "The mitochondria isolated following the protocol described here, are highly active and capable of DNA, RNA and protein synthesis."
3. "Mitochondrial tRNA aminoacylation, mtDNA–protein interactions and specific import of added proteins into the organelles, can also be studied using this kind of preparations."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides an update on a fast and simple method for isolating mammalian mitochondria. The isolated mitochondria are highly active and capable of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis. The method allows for the study of various aspects of mitochondrial biogenesis. However, the article does not provide the specific steps of the protocol, only mentioning its fast and simple nature. Further details of the isolation protocol are not provided, leaving gaps in the complete understanding of the procedure.

summary:
Iterative Summary 3: Isolation of mitochondria for biogenetical studies: An update

Researchers from the Universidad de Zaragoza and Fondazione IRCCS Istituto Neurologico ‘‘C.Besta’’ have developed a fast and simple method for purifying mammalian mitochondria. The isolated mitochondria are highly active and capable of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis. By studying mitochondrial tRNA aminoacylation, mtDNA-protein interactions, and specific protein import, researchers gain insights into organelle biogenesis. Mitochondrial dysfunction, associated with neurodegenerative diseases, diabetes, and cancer, remains poorly understood. Isolated mitochondria provide a valuable tool for investigating the OXPHOS system biogenesis and metabolic processes, shedding light on the underlying mechanisms of these pathologies. The experimental conditions and the thyroid state of the animal influence the outcomes of mitochondrial research. This study was conducted by Erika Fernández-Vizarra, Gustavo Ferrín, Acisclo Pérez-Martos, Patricio Fernández-Silva, Massimo Zeviani, and José Antonio Enríquez. The research was supported by the Departamento de Bioquímica y Biología Molecular y Celular, Universidad de Zaragoza, Pedro Cerbuna 12, 50009 Zaragoza, Spain, UO Neurogenetica Molecolare, Fondazione IRCCS Istituto Neurologico ‘‘C.Besta ’, Via Temolo 4, 20126 Milano, Italy, and Centro Nacional de Investigaciones Cardiovasculares Carlos III, Melchor Fernández Almagro, 3, 28029 Madrid, Spain. This article was published by Elsevier B.V and Mitochondria Research Society.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. The organs of interest (liver and kidney) are extirpated and rinsed with cold homogenization medium (0.32 M sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, and 10 mM Tris–HCl, pH 7.4).
2. The organs are cut into small pieces and washed to remove blood and connective tissue using a metal sieve and fresh homogenization medium.
3. The cut and clean tissue is transferred to a glass Elvehjem potter with fresh homogenization medium in a proportion of 4 ml/g of liver or 5 ml/g of kidney.
4. Homogenization is performed using a motor-driven Teflon pestle with 4 up and down strokes at 600 rpm.
5. The homogenate is transferred to a 50 ml centrifuge tube and spun at 1000 g for 5 min at 4°C to sediment unbroken tissue, cells, and nuclei.
6. The supernatant (S1) is transferred to eight 1.5 ml-Eppendorf tubes, centrifuged in a microfuge at 15,000 g for 2 min at 4°C.
7. The supernatant is removed, carefully eliminating all the fat and the light-colored fluffy layer on the top of the darker pellet containing the mitochondria.
8. Homogenization medium is added to half of the tubes, and the contents of two Eppendorf tubes are combined and resuspended together in 1.5 ml of medium.
9. Mitochondria are resuspended gently using glass Pasteur pipettes with a bulb.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide information on the specific volumes of homogenization medium used for washing the organs or the specific volumes of medium added to the Eppendorf tubes.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from liver and kidney tissues. However, there are missing details regarding the specific volumes of homogenization medium used for washing the organs and the specific volumes of medium added to the Eppendorf tubes. These missing details should be addressed to ensure the reproducibility of the protocol.

summary: Summary 4: The Hospital Universitario Reina Sofía de Córdoba in Córdoba, Spain, conducts biogenetical analysis of the OXPHOS system using high quality mitochondrial preparations. The method involves isolating functional mitochondria from mammalian tissues and cultured cells using a homogenization method. The crude mitochondrial fraction is obtained through differential centrifugation and then washed to eliminate contaminants. The resulting mitochondrial preparations have been widely used for studying various processes involved in the biogenesis of the OXPHOS system, including mtDNA replication, transcription, tRNA aminoacylation, translation, protein import, enzymatic activity measurements, and protein interaction with mtDNA. The isolated fractions can be used for analysis of mitochondrial DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis, as well as tRNA aminoacylation and protein import assays. The procedure can be adapted for different sources of mitochondria, and several research groups have successfully used this protocol for high-quality mitochondrial preparations.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The final pellets are equilibrated in 1 ml of the appropriate buffer for the experiment to be performed, pelleted again, and resuspended in 1 ml of the final buffer.
2. The resuspended mitochondria are maintained in a water-ice bath until used.
3. In the case of liver and kidney mitochondria, the direct transfer of the S1 supernatant to the Eppendorf tubes yields enough mitochondria for most purposes.
4. A series of washes are efficient for the elimination of a significant amount of contaminating particles that contain high concentrations of harmful enzymes such as nucleases, phospholipases, and proteases.
5. The yield of these mitochondrial preparations with respect to the wet weight of starting tissue is estimated as approximately 6 mg mitochondrial protein per g of liver or kidney.
6. The protocol to extract heart mitochondria begins following the same steps described above.
7. The homogenization medium utilized is medium AT (0.075 M sucrose, 0.225 M sorbitol, 1 mM EGTA, 0.1% fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin (BSA), and 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4).
8. The washed and minced tissue is homogenized in 10 ml medium/g heart to be centrifuged at low speed (1000g for 5 min at 4°C).
9. The resulting supernatant is transferred to a clean tube to be spun at high speed (9000g for 10 min at 4°C).
10. The mitochondrial pellet (P2) obtained in this high-speed centrifuge is enriched in nucleases, making it not suitable for in organello transcription assays.
11. To clean the P2 pellet, it is resuspended with 5 ml of homogenization medium AT and then transferred to four 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes and washed to obtain a single pellet.
12. These tubes are centrifuged in a microfuge at 15,000g for 2 min at 4°C.
13. The washed mitochondria are equilibrated and resuspended in the appropriate volume of the final buffer.
14. The heart of a 250-g rat weighs between 1 and 1.5 g.
15. The steps to extract mitochondria from the brain are essentially the same as for the heart.
16. The only difference is that the brain is homogenized with 10-15 strokes using a Dounce-type glass homogenizer with a manually driven glass pestle, adding 5 ml of homogenization medium AT per g of starting material.

**Conclusion:** The article provides detailed steps for isolating mitochondria from liver, kidney, heart, and brain. The protocol for liver and kidney mitochondria includes multiple washes to eliminate contaminating particles, while the heart and brain protocols follow similar steps with minor differences. However, the article does not provide information on the specific buffers used for equilibration or the final buffer. Additionally, the protocol lacks information on the centrifugation speeds and times for the initial low-speed and high-speed spins.

Mitochondria are isolated from various mouse organs, including liver, kidney, heart, and brain, using centrifugation and washing steps. Liver and kidney mitochondria can be obtained by directly transferring the supernatant to Eppendorf tubes. The washing steps remove contaminants and harmful enzymes. The P2 pellet obtained from high-speed centrifugation contains nucleases and is unsuitable for organello transcription assays. The yield of mitochondrial preparations is estimated to be approximately 6 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram of liver or kidney tissue. Microsomes and lysosomes were isolated from liver and kidney tissues. The extraction of heart mitochondria involved the use of medium AT as the homogenization medium. Brain mitochondria were extracted using a Dounce-type glass homogenizer. The yield of heart and brain mitochondrial preparations is lower compared to liver and kidney, but they are purer and highly functional. The heart of a 250-g rat weighs between 1 and 1.5 g, requiring three or four animals to obtain a sufficient amount of starting material. The brain was homogenized with 10-15 strokes for further analysis.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for isolating mitochondria from solid tissue:
1. Resuspend and rehomogenize the first low-speed pellet (P1).
2. Centrifuge the resuspended P1 under the same conditions as for solid tissue.
3. Combine the two low-speed supernatants (S1a and S1b) and centrifuge at high speed (9000g).
4. Wash the mitochondria contained in the high-speed pellet (P2) in Eppendorf tubes.
5. If separation of free mitochondria and synaptosomes is needed, use an aqueous two-phase partition system composed of Dextran T 500 and Polyethylene Glycol 4000.

Steps for isolating mitochondria from cultured mammalian cells:
1. Harvest cells by trypsinization and pellet them at 600g for 5 min.
2. Wash the cell pellets twice with a cold isotonic medium (e.g., PBS or NKM).
3. Break the cell pellets by adding hypotonic homogenization buffer (IB 0.1x) and homogenize with a Thomas homogenizer.
4. Add hypertonic buffer (IB 10 /C2) to make the medium isotonic.
5. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1200g for 3 min at 4°C to pellet unbroken cells, debris, and nuclei.
6. Collect the supernatant and centrifuge it again at low speed (1200g for 3 min at 4°C) to eliminate heavy contaminants.
7. Optionally, perform a second homogenization and centrifugation of the first low-speed pellet to increase the yield of mitochondria.
8. Combine the first and second low-speed supernatants and pellet the mitochondria in Eppendorf tubes by centrifugation.
9. Wash the mitochondrial pellets using homogenization buffer A and join the pellets together in a single tube.
10. Equilibrate and resuspend the final mitochondrial pellet in the appropriate buffer and keep it on ice until used.

Conclusion:
The protocol for isolating mitochondria from solid tissue is described in detail, including the additional step of resuspending and rehomogenizing the first low-speed pellet. However, there is a gap in the protocol regarding the washing steps for the high-speed pellet. The protocol for isolating mitochondria from cultured mammalian cells is also described in detail, with all the necessary steps provided. Overall, the protocol for isolating mitochondria from solid tissue is complete, while the protocol for isolating mitochondria from cultured mammalian cells is mostly complete with a minor gap in the washing steps for the high-speed pellet.

summary:
Summary 4: To increase mitochondrial yield from exponentially growing cultured cells, such as HeLa cells, the cells are harvested by trypsinization, pelleted, and washed with a cold isotonic medium. They are then broken using hypotonic homogenization buffer and centrifuged to remove unbroken cells, debris, and nuclei. A second homogenization and centrifugation of the first low-speed pellet can be performed to increase mitochondrial yield. The resulting low-speed supernatant(s) are pelleted in Eppendorf tubes using a microfuge. The pellets are washed using homogenization buffer A and then combined in a single tube. The final mitochondrial pellet is equilibrated and resuspended in the appropriate buffer.

The functionality of isolated mitochondria can be assessed by measuring oxygen consumption rate, Respiratory Control Ratio (RCR), and P/O ratio using an oxygen electrode. RCR values higher than 4 and P/O ratios approaching 3 indicate tightly coupled mitochondria. Mitochondrial fractions prepared using these protocols show good coupling.

The yield and purity of mitochondrial preparations can be evaluated based on relative enrichment in mitochondria and the presence of contaminants. It is important to prevent the presence of fatty acids during the purification steps, especially when isolating mitochondria from heart and brain, by using homogenization media containing fatty acid-free BSA.

This method is suitable for any type of cultured cells, including primary fibroblast cultures. Several studies, including those by Whittaker, Lopez-Perez, Enriquez and Attardi, Fernandez-Vizarra et al., Gaines and Attardi, Frezza et al., Rickwood et al., and Moreno-Loshuertos et al., have contributed to the development and refinement of this method.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Enrichment of mitochondria is usually monitored by measuring the activities of the mitochondrial matrix enzyme citrate synthase (CS) or the inner membrane cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV).
2. The most common contaminants in mitochondrial preparations are lysosomes, microsomes (endoplasmic reticulum), and peroxisomes. Enzymatic assays can be used to evaluate these contaminants, such as acid phosphatase activity for lysosomes, glucose-6-phosphatase activity for endoplasmic reticulum, and catalase activity for peroxisomes.
3. Electron microscopy morphometric analysis can be used to estimate unidentified contaminants for which no enzymatic marker is available.
4. Specific contaminants for certain types of preparations, such as myelin membrane debris in brain crude mitochondrial preparations, can be detected by monitoring 20,30-cyclic nucleotide 30-phosphohydrolase activity.
5. Additional purification steps using density gradient procedures may reduce the presence of contaminants, but these methods are laborious, time-consuming, and may affect the functionality of the purified organelles.
6. In all assays, a multi-purpose incubation buffer called MAITE is used. The solution's composition includes sucrose, sorbitol, KCl, EDTA, MgCl2, Tris-HCl, H3PO4, and Tris base to adjust the pH.
7. Mitochondria are incubated at a concentration of 1-2 mg mitochondrial protein/ml at 37°C using a rotary shaker for variable periods of time, depending on the assay.
8. Sedimentation of mitochondria during the incubation period must be avoided to prevent irreversible damage due to lack of oxygenation.
9. To prevent uncoupling of mitochondria, fatty acid-free BSA is added to the incubation mixture.
10. ATP is generated by adding ADP, glutamate, and malate to the medium. The intramitochondrial ATP concentration influences the replication and expression of mtDNA.
11. Different hormones, drugs, etc., can be added to the incubation mixture to evaluate their effects on mitochondrial DNA expression.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. "Crude brain mitochondrial preparations, that contain synapto-somes, are suitable for in organello replication analyses (as well as for transcription, see below), when radiolabeled nucleotides are used, since these do not cross the plasma membrane of synap-tosomes."
2. "To avoid this background, it is recommended that DNase or micrococcal nuclease (both from Roche Applied Science) is added during the mtDNA replication assay, in this way only mtDNA is labeled."
3. "The nucleic acids are extracted from the isolated mitochondria by incubating them in the presence of pronase (Roche Applied Science) and SDS, followed by phenol extraction and ethanol precipitation."
4. "The radiolabeled mtDNA is analyzed by agarose gel electrophoresis and autoradiography."
5. "When the samples are run in native conditions, the labeled mitochondrial DNA is resolved into several bands that correspond to the different conformations of the closed mtDNA circles."
6. "The expected size fragments corresponding to the human mitochondrial genome appear when the samples are digested with the restriction enzyme ApaI, confirming that what has been de novo synthesized in the isolated mitochondria is indeed mtDNA."
7. "Similar incubation conditions are used for the in organello transcription analyses."
8. "The addition of 20 lCi of 400–600 Ci/mmol [ a-32P]UTP is sufficient to maintain the transcriptional activity of rat liver, heart, brain, and cultured cell-derived mitochondria without the supply of additional nucleotides."
9. "However, when using other sources of mitochondria, for instance, kidney-derived organelles, it is recommended to supply the incubation medium with 50 lM CTP."
10. "The samples are treated with DDM (Bcs1 l) and digitonin (Uqcrfs1) and run through first dimension (1D) BNGE."
11. "The pattern obtained in the autoradiogram is compared with the same Coomassie-stained gel."
12. "Autoradiograms corresponding to the same samples as in (B) run through a second dimension (2-D) denaturing Tricine–SDS–PAGE after the 1D native gel."

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation for in organello replication and transcription analyses. The steps include the use of radiolabeled nucleotides, addition of DNase or micrococcal nuclease to avoid background labeling, extraction of nucleic acids from isolated mitochondria, analysis of radiolabeled mtDNA using agarose gel electrophoresis and autoradiography, digestion of mtDNA with a restriction enzyme, and incubation conditions for in organello transcription analyses. The article also mentions the use of DDM and digitonin treatment and gel electrophoresis for import assays. However, some details, such as the specific incubation conditions and additional steps, are not provided.

Electrophoretic patterns of mtRNAs synthesized in isolated mitochondria from various tissues and cells were analyzed. Electrophoretic patterns of labeled mitochondrial proteins were observed using mitochondria from different sources. The OXPHOS system complexes were resolved using Blue-Native Gel Electrophoresis and denaturing Tricine-SDS-PAGE. The incorporation of mitochondrial proteins into complexes was tracked using pulse-chase experiments. In organello replication analyses were conducted using crude brain mitochondrial preparations. DNase or micrococcal nuclease was added to avoid labeling non-mitochondrial DNA. Nucleic acids were extracted from isolated mitochondria using pronase and SDS. The radiolabeled mtDNA was analyzed using agarose gel electrophoresis and autoradiography. In organello transcription analyses were performed with incubation times ranging from 1 to 3 hours. The addition of [a-32P]UTP maintained the transcriptional activity of mitochondria from rat liver, heart, brain, and cultured cells. Kidney-derived organelles required the addition of CTP to the incubation medium. In organello import assays were conducted using radiolabeled proteins and isolated mitochondria. The position of complexes or supercomplexes was determined using BNGE and denaturing Tricine-SDS-PAGE. RCR and P/O ratios were measured in mitochondrial preparations from different sources.

7S RNA as well as the precursors for the COI mRNA (p-COI) and for

and are used by their mitochondria In our hands, no detectable
aminoacylation of cytoplasmic tRNAs has been observed when

summary: Mitochondrial transcriptional performance can be improved in kidney mitochondria by adding 10 mM succinate instead of glutamate. Cold UTP chase after labeling enhances results in heart mitochondria. Contaminant nucleo-cytoplasmic fractions do not exhibit transcriptional activity in any tested mitochondrial source. Mitochondrial RNA is extracted using pronase and analyzed by electrophoresis and autoradiography. Methylmercury hydroxide is used for denaturation. In organello tRNA aminoacylation is performed using MAITE medium with labeled amino acids. In organello protein synthesis is conducted in the presence of all 20 L-amino acids, with emetine and cycloheximide added to inhibit cytoplasmic protein synthesis. Radiolabeled polypeptides are detected through SDS-PAGE. In organello protein synthesis yields a similar pattern to in vivo labeling. Chunk # 7.
In organello protein import
Isolated mitochondria extracted from tissues and cultured cells
following the described protocol, are able to import mitochondrial
pre-proteins synthesized in vitro using rabbit reticulocyte lysate
systems (Promega) (Ryan et al., 2001 ) Usually the mitochondrial
protein precursor is radiolabeled using [
35S]methionine for its
detection In the import assays, mitochondria are incubated inMAITE medium up to a ﬁnal volume of 50
ll in the presence of
1 mg/ml bovine serum albumin (BSA), 2 mM Sodium Succinate,1 mM ATP (note that ATP concentrations higher than 4 mM will
inhibit import) and 1 mM of the cold amino acid used to label
the precursor protein, included to avoid labeling of newly synthe-
sized proteins by mitochondria using the remaining radioactive
amino acid With the same purpose, 40
lg/ml chloramphenicol
(Sigma–Aldrich) can also be included in the incubation medium.Cycloheximide and/or emetine may also be added to avoid back-
ground due to nucleo-cytoplasmic residual protein synthesis activ-
ity Finally, 5
ll (10% (v/v) of the mitochondrial suspension) of the
reticulocyte lysate containing the labeled precursor is added andthe reactions are incubated at 37 /C176C for 30–60 min in a rotating
wheel To determine whether the import process is dependent on
the mitochondrial membrane potential, a mitochondrial uncoupler
such as valinomycin (10–20
lM; Sigma–Aldrich) or CCCP (1.7 lg/
ml; Sigma–Aldrich) can be added to the reaction mix (Fig 5 A) As
a control for the import reactions, mitochondria can be incubated
in the presence of a protease (such as trypsin or proteinase K) to
determine if the protein is protected from the protease activity
which would indicate that it has actually been internalized For the detection of the imported radiolabeled protein, mitochon-
dria are lysed under denaturing conditions and run through SDS–
PAGE ( Fig 5 A) When using brain mitochondrial fractions, it is
important to know that peptides do not cross the synaptosomalmembranes These assays are interesting to determine whether a protein is
indeed mitochondrial, if it is imported into mitochondria (Fernan-
dez-Silva et al., 1997; Fernandez-Vizarra et al., 2007a; Ghezzi et al.,
2008; Petruzzella et al., 1998; Spinazzola et al., 2006 ) and which
pathway is used to internalize it ( Bolender et al., 2008; Wiede-
mann et al., 2004 ) In Fig 5 A, two examples of import assays for260 E Fernández-Vizarra et al / Mitochondrion 10 (2010) 253–262two different proteins are shown The radiolabeled precursors of
the mouse CI assembly factor Cia30 (Dunning et al., 2007; Vogel
et al., 2005 ), an inner-membrane protein, and of a human TFAM-
GFP fusion protein, a matrix protein, are internalized into mito-chondria and are processed giving rise to the smaller mature forms The mechanism is dependent on the mitochondrial membrane po-
tential because the processing does not take place in the presence
of the uncoupler CCCP The mature (processed) proteins are pro-
tected from protease digestion by the mitochondrial membranes The incorporation of imported polypeptides, namely nuclearly-
encoded OXPHOS complex subunits or any other mitochondrial in-
ner-membrane protein, into a higher molecular weight complex
can be followed if after the import reaction the mitochondria are
treated for and electrophoresed in Blue-Native gels These types
of analyses have been used to study the assembly of nuclearly-en-coded Complex I structural subunits and assembly factors (Lazarou
et al., 2007 ).Fig 5 B and C show the incorporation of the radiola-
beled Complex III assembly factor Bcs1 l into a high molecularweight complex that is smaller than CIII (Fernandez-Vizarra
et al., 2007b ) and of the structural subunit Rieske protein (Uqcrfs1)
into free CIII and the CIII associated with other OXPHOS complexes(supercomplexes), by using mitochondrial fractions prepared as
described before 5.6 In organello mtDNA footprinting
It is possible to study the interaction of proteins with mtDNA
using isolated mitochondria by methylation interference assays
(Enriquez et al., 1999a; Madsen et al., 1996; Micol et al., 1997 ) Mitochondria are incubated, in the same conditions as for the otherdescribed experiments, for 30 min and in the presence of BSA,
glutamate, malate and ADP Subsequently, dimethyl sulfate
(DMS; Sigma–Aldrich) is added to a ﬁnal concentration of 0.1%
and allowed to react for 2 min at 37 /C176C, and the mixture is quickly
cooled on ice Mitochondria are washed and mtDNA is isolated asdescribed above After the methylation, the mtDNA is cleaved
using piperidine (Sigma–Aldrich) and the area of interest is
analyzed by primer-extension To determine the protected or
hyper-methylated mtDNA areas indicating DNA–protein interac-
tions, the results of the incubated mitochondria are compared with
DMS-treated naked mtDNA (Madsen et al., 1996; Micol et al.,
1997 ) 6

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation in organello protein import:

1. Isolate mitochondria from tissues and cultured cells following the described protocol.
2. Radiolabel the mitochondrial protein precursor using [35S]methionine.
3. Prepare the import assay medium by adding the following components to the mitochondria: 
   - 1 mg/ml bovine serum albumin (BSA)
   - 2 mM Sodium Succinate
   - 1 mM ATP (note: ATP concentrations higher than 4 mM will inhibit import)
   - 1 mM of the cold amino acid used to label the precursor protein (to avoid labeling of newly synthesized proteins)
   - 40 μg/ml chloramphenicol (optional, to prevent residual protein synthesis activity)
   - Cycloheximide and/or emetine (optional, to avoid background due to nucleo-cytoplasmic residual protein synthesis activity)
4. Add 5 μl (10% v/v) of the reticulocyte lysate containing the labeled precursor to the mitochondrial suspension.
5. Incubate the reactions at 37°C for 30-60 minutes in a rotating wheel.
6. To determine the dependence of the import process on the mitochondrial membrane potential, add a mitochondrial uncoupler such as valinomycin or CCCP to the reaction mix.
7. As a control, incubate mitochondria in the presence of a protease (such as trypsin or proteinase K) to determine if the protein is protected from protease activity, indicating internalization.
8. To detect the imported radiolabeled protein, lyse the mitochondria under denaturing conditions and run through SDS-PAGE.
9. For brain mitochondrial fractions, note that peptides do not cross the synaptosomal membranes.
10. These assays are useful for determining if a protein is mitochondrial, if it is imported into mitochondria, and which pathway is used for internalization.

Incompleteness:
- The article does not provide the complete protocol for isolating mitochondria.
- The specific composition of the import assay medium is not provided (e.g., the complete list of components and their concentrations).
- The duration of the incubation with dimethyl sulfate for mtDNA footprinting is not specified.

Conclusion:
The steps provided in the article describe the process of mitochondrial isolation for organello protein import. However, there are missing details, such as the complete protocol for isolating mitochondria and the specific composition of the import assay medium. Additionally, the duration of the incubation for mtDNA footprinting is not specified. These gaps in the protocol would need to be addressed to replicate the experiment accurately.

Import assays are conducted in the presence of specific components and conditions to facilitate protein import into mitochondria. The imported proteins are protected from protease activity and undergo processing. The incorporation of imported polypeptides into higher molecular weight complexes can be analyzed using Blue-Native gels. Additionally, methylation interference assays can be performed to study protein-DNA interactions with mtDNA using isolated mitochondria.

Chunk # 8.
Final remarks
We have presented here a method for the extraction of mito-
chondria that can be useful for many applications and that can
be advantageous compared to other protocols because: (i) the
mitochondrial fractions are obtained quickly, (ii) the method is
gentle, yielding highly functional mitochondria and (iii) the ob-
tained preparations are pure enough to be used in many analyses,
in a very controlled environment, of different aspects of mitochon-
drial biogenesis Acknowledgements
